title: Incident Bot v2 operator guide + troubleshooting (deploy/config diff/saturation cards)
preview: This PR updates the operator-facing documentation for Incident Bot v2 diagnostics (GA-ready). The goal is to make the bot usable in the first 5 minutes of an incident by standardizing what each context card means, how to validate it, and what to do when it’s missing/empty due to permissions or upstream timeouts. The guide also documents degraded-mode behavior (cached context + links) and the security model for tenant-safe config diffs (redaction + audit logging + break-glass). Scope: docs only (no product/runtime changes). Motivation: recent incident retros and the beta rotation feedback showed that responders could see the cards but weren’t sure what thresholds/links were authoritative, and we repeatedly re-taught the same troubleshooting steps in #help. This adds a single canonical “Operator guide” that Support/SRE/On-call can link during active incidents. Contents: (1) Overview of v2 cards (Recent deploys / Config diff / Saturation signals / Error budget burn links). (2) Interpretation + common pitfalls: staged rollouts, partial propagation, canary vs full, tenant pinning, routing policy interactions, and why GPU% alone is not actionable without queue depth and admission/backpressure. (3) Troubleshooting playbook: empty cards, permission denied, redaction warnings, telemetry query timeouts, and how to switch to link-only mode. (4) Deep link standards into Console (service/region/time window/tenant safe params) and how to widen/narrow time ranges. (5) Security notes: what the bot is allowed to show in Slack, how to request elevated access, and what audit events are emitted. (6) Rollout notes: feature-flag cohorts, GA announcement template, and rollback criteria (docs references only; implementation lives elsewhere).

title: Add TP stall dashboard panels + alert rules (heartbeat gaps, stall reasons, timeout correlation)
preview:
Context
We’ve had repeated Dedicated + high-throughput Hosted incidents where TP replicas appear healthy at the process level but are effectively wedged: requests hang, streaming timeouts rise, and autoscaling thrashes. Runtime-side instrumentation is landing in parallel (ENG-4823 / runtime PRs), but we need a first-class dashboard + alerting layer to detect silent stalls early and make triage actionable.

This PR adds:
- Grafana dashboard panels for TP heartbeat gaps (per rank + aggregated), stall reason counts, and request timeout correlation by model/cluster/replica.
- Prometheus alert rules with sane defaults + paging policy alignment (page only on sustained stall/timeout correlation, ticket otherwise).
- Links from alerts to the on-call runbook and to the most relevant filters (cluster/account/model).

What’s included (panels)
1) TP Heartbeat
- Panel: “TP heartbeat age (p50/p95/p99) by model + cluster”
- Panel: “Max heartbeat age per replica (topk)” to quickly identify the worst offenders
- Panel: “Heartbeat gap events / min” derived from heartbeat age crossing threshold
- Variables: env, region, cluster, namespace, model, tp_degree, replica_id

2) Stall reasons
- Panel: “TP stall reasons (rate)” stacked by reason_code (compute | comms | scheduler_starvation | kv_compaction | unknown)
- Panel: “In-flight collectives by type” to distinguish ‘stuck in collective’ vs ‘no progress / no collectives’
- Panel: “Scheduler queue depth + oldest age” for starvation diagnosis

3) Request impact + correlation
- Panel: “Request timeout rate (5xx + upstream timeout) by route/model”
- Panel: “Streaming disconnects/timeouts vs TP heartbeat gaps” (dual-axis)
- Panel: “Top replicas by timeout correlation score” (uses a simple z-score vs baseline window; see implementation note)

Alert rules
- TPStallHeartbeatGapPage (PAGE)
  - Trigger: heartbeat_age_seconds p99 > 30s for 10m AND request_timeouts_rate > baseline+X for same model/cluster
  - Routing: pager to eng-sre, notify eng-runtime channel
  - Runbook: runtime-tp-hang-mitigation (Confluence)
- TPStallHeartbeatGapTicket (TICKET)
  - Trigger: heartbeat_age_seconds p95 > 15s for 20m without timeout correlation (early warning)
- TPStallReasonSpike (TICKET)
  - Trigger: stall_reason_rate{reason_code!="unknown"} spikes above threshold for 15m

Implementation notes
- This assumes the runtime exports (or will soon export) the following series (names aligned with the metric naming thread):
  - redwood_runtime_tp_rank_heartbeat_age_seconds{cluster,model,replica_id,rank}
  - redwood_runtime_tp_stall_reason_total{cluster,model,replica_id,reason_code}
  - redwood_runtime_tp_collectives_in_flight{cluster,model,replica_id,collective}
  - redwood_runtime_scheduler_queue_depth{cluster,model,replica_id,queue}
- For correlation panels we use existing gateway/request metrics:
  - redwood_gateway_request_timeouts_total{route,model,cluster}
  - redwood_gateway_streaming_disconnects_total{route,model,cluster,reason}
- If a metric isn’t present in a given environment, panels will render “No data” rather than hard failing (Grafana transformations updated accordingly).

Files / areas changed
- dashboards/tp-stall-and-timeout.jsonnet: new dashboard + variables + panels
- alerts/tp-stall-alerts.yaml: new alert groups and annotations
- mixins/runtime-mixin.libsonnet: wires dashboard + rules into mixin build
- docs/dashboards/tp-stall-and-timeout.md: short operator docs + common queries
- docs/alerts/paging-policy.md: adds rationale for page vs ticket split

Screenshots
- Added screenshots in the PR comments (Grafana ‘view’ links) for the main dashboard and the three alert panels.

Testing / verification
- jsonnetfmt + grafonnet compile
- promtool check rules
- Deployed to staging Grafana (obs-staging) and validated:
  - variables filter as expected
  - alert expressions return expected series for a known test TP job
  - annotations render with runbook + dashboard links

Rollout plan
- Merge -> auto-publish to obs-staging
- After 24h of staging validation, promote to prod dashboards
- Enable paging rule initially in ‘warn’ mode for 48h (no page), then flip to page once thresholds are confirmed with SRE (Rafael/Aisha)

Links
- Linear: ENG-4823 (metrics), ENG-4828 (perf canary scenarios), ENG-4821 (initiative)
- Internal support: INT-2219 (enable extra debug logging during canary; referenced for runbook correlation)
- Runbook: Confluence /eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/runtime-tp-hang-mitigation

Checklist
- [x] Dashboard renders in staging
- [x] Alert rules validated with promtool
- [x] Annotations include runbook + dashboard deep links
- [x] Paging policy reviewed with SRE

Notes / follow-ups
- Once runtime PR adds collective counters to tracing spans (ENG-4823 follow-on), we should add a trace-link panel to jump from a stall window to exemplar trace IDs (Jacob: tracking separately).

title: Add Dedicated staged rollout dashboards + alert templates (stage p95/p99, error rate, GPU saturation, promotion annotations)
preview:
Summary: This PR adds a first cut of dashboard + alert templates for the Dedicated staged rollout system. The dashboards are stage-aware (stage_id / stage_name) and are designed to support the rollout gate model in the policy engine (latency, errors, saturation/headroom, GPU health proxies). It also adds annotations for stage promotion/abort/hold events so operators can correlate metric shifts with rollout actions.

Context / dependencies: This is intended to ship alongside the redwood monorepo changes that (a) emit stage tags in metrics/traces and (b) provide audit events for promote/abort/hold. Dashboards assume the following labels are present on the relevant metrics: dedicated_pool_id, rollout_plan_id, stage_id, stage_name, region. For cardinality safety, the dashboards intentionally avoid any per-request labels and only group-by the allowlisted stage identifiers.

What’s included:
- Grafana dashboards (templated) for Dedicated staged rollouts:
  1) "Dedicated Rollouts: Stage Overview" (default landing)
     - Stage timeline annotations (promote/abort/hold)
     - p95/p99 latency per stage (overall + per region)
     - request rate + error rate (5xx + 429 separately)
     - saturation/headroom proxy panels (GPU utilization, queue depth)
     - OOM / throttling proxy panels (where available)
  2) "Dedicated Rollouts: Stage Drilldown" (single stage focus)
     - gating window panels (5m + 15m, side-by-side)
     - sample size panel (req/min, tokens/sec) to reduce false confidence
     - region comparison table with pass/fail indicator driven by threshold variables
  3) "Dedicated Rollouts: Fleet Signals" (operator/SRE)
     - node/GPU health summary by pool + region
     - ECC error rate proxy + throttling counters (when present)
- Alert templates (Prometheus rules) aligned to v1 gates:
  - GateCandidateHighErrorRate (per stage)
  - GateCandidateP99LatencyRegression (per stage)
  - GateCandidateHigh429Rate (per stage; motivated by early canary starving capacity)
  - GateCandidateGPUSaturation (per stage + per region)
  - GateCandidateQueueDepthSpike (per stage)
  - TelemetryStaleForStage (marks "unknown" gate evidence scenarios; ticket-level)
- Annotations:
  - Adds Grafana annotation queries backed by the audit event stream (via Loki index) for: rollout.plan.created, rollout.stage.promoted, rollout.stage.aborted, rollout.stage.held, rollout.stage.force_promoted.
  - Annotation text includes actor + reason (if provided) and links back to the Console rollout page (URL templated).

Notes on thresholds (v1):
- Thresholds are intentionally conservative and are parameterized as Grafana variables where possible so we can tune by tier without re-cutting dashboards.
- We split 429 from 5xx because 429 is the most common early-stage failure signature when partition sizing is too small.
- Missing data: dashboards show "no data" explicitly; alerts include a separate TelemetryStaleForStage rule instead of failing other rules open/closed.

Validation:
- Tested with staging data using synthetic stage tags and replayed a known canary incident window from SUP-28522 to confirm panels highlight 429 + queue depth.
- Verified annotations render correctly with sample audit events and do not explode cardinality.

Rollout plan:
- Land dashboards behind the dedicated_rollouts folder; do not set as default home.
- SRE to enable alert rules for staging first, then production after one week of tuning.

Follow-ups (not in this PR):
- Add a customer-facing pared-down dashboard variant (hides infra-only signals).
- Add quality regression panels (eval scores) once the stage-tagged eval pipeline is finalized.
- Tighten region impairment visualization (heatmap) after we confirm multi-region rollout behavior in beta.

Screenshots (staging):
- https://grafana.staging.redwood.example/d/dedicated-rollouts-stage-overview
- https://grafana.staging.redwood.example/d/dedicated-rollouts-stage-drilldown

References:
- Linear: ENG-5966 (stage-tagged metrics), PM-827 (default thresholds)
- Support: SUP-28522 (canary 429s), SUP-28605 (missing gate evidence)
- Related code: redwood PRs for stage tags + audit events (see internal rollout doc for exact SHAs).

title: Dedicated throughput SLO: add dashboard panels for floor tracking, saturation, and drilldowns
preview:
Context: We’re rolling out a formal Dedicated throughput SLO and need a consistent, low-friction dashboard experience for SRE/CS and (later) a curated subset for customers. This PR extends the dedicated mixin dashboards with panels that (a) track throughput floors over time per customer/deployment, (b) show saturation signals that correlate with SLO misses, and (c) provide drilldowns into the exact deployment/model/region slice.

What’s included: 1) “Throughput floor vs actual” panels: tokens/sec (per deployment_id) overlaid with computed floor (tier + shape) and SLO evaluator compliance; includes 5m/30m rollups with clear “below floor” highlighting. 2) “Saturation signals” row: admission-control shed rate, queue depth, in-flight requests, batcher wait time, KV cache hit ratio (when available), and GPU utilization proxy (SM occupancy / active time from node exporter where present). 3) Drilldowns: links from customer-level panels → deployment-level panels, and deployment-level → explore queries + logs/traces (requires `deployment_id` label) with pre-filled time range and filters. 4) Variables & filtering: standardized template variables (`customer_id`, `deployment_id`, `tier`, `region`, `model_family`) and a default view that emphasizes Dedicated pools only. 5) Runbook hooks: adds inline links to the Dedicated throughput SLO escalation runbook and the tier-aware routing/overload protection runbook.

Query semantics: Uses the canonical per-deployment throughput metric emitted by runtime (`redwood_inference_tokens_total` / `rate`), with streaming-aware token accounting (relies on the runtime fix merged separately; this PR avoids older `stream_tokens_total` series). Floor overlay is sourced from the SLO evaluator summary series (`dedicated_throughput_floor_tokens_per_sec`) when available; falls back to a static tier mapping panel note when the evaluator series is missing in a region.

Screenshots: Not attached; dashboards can be rendered locally via `make dashboards` and compared in Grafana using the “mixin-preview” folder output.

Rollout: Safe to merge; panels are behind the existing Dedicated dashboard folder and only appear where the required metrics exist. Follow-up: once the customer-facing Console widget lands, we’ll likely want a simplified “status” view fed by the same evaluator series.

title: Dedicated throughput SLO: add burn-rate alerts + wire to paging policies
preview: Context: As part of Dedicated throughput guarantees, we need first-class SLO alerting that is (a) burn-rate based (fast/slow), (b) scoped per Dedicated deployment, and (c) correctly routed to oncall + CS escalation paths. This PR adds burn-rate alert rules for the Dedicated Throughput Floor SLO and wires them into our paging policies. It also adds the runbook links and dashboard deep-links to make the alerts actionable. Scope: - New Prometheus rules for Dedicated throughput SLO burn-rate (page + ticket) - Tier-aware routing labels (Standard/Plus/Premium) to support different urgency/paging - Alertmanager routes + PagerDuty service mapping (SRE oncall; non-page tickets to #customer-success triage) - Annotations: customer/deployment identifiers, region, model, and links to the Dedicated Throughput dashboard panels - Guardrails: suppress alerts when deployment is in provisioning/warmup window or explicitly marked 'customer_throttled' by admission-control telemetry Notes: The burn-rate computations follow the SLO Toolkit conventions (fast: 2h window, slow: 24h window) but are implemented using Redwood’s in-repo rule templates to avoid an additional dependency at runtime. Related: ENG-19244 (metric emission), ENG-19257 (tier-aware admission control).

title: Runtime: safer TP defaults (concurrency + batch guardrails) behind feature flag
preview:
Title: Runtime: safer TP defaults (concurrency + batch guardrails) behind feature flag

Context
We have repeated reports of TP replicas hanging under sustained mixed prefill/decode + bursty concurrency (see SUP-48321, SUP-48344; also linked to ongoing ENG-4821/ENG-4824 work). While root-cause fixes are landing (scheduler ordering, NCCL stream sync, watchdog), we still need a low-risk operational mitigation we can roll out quickly to Dedicated canaries.

This PR introduces a feature-flagged guardrail set for TP replicas that reduces the probability of starvation / collective mismatch scenarios by:
- capping concurrent decode microbatches per TP replica
- limiting prefill batch tokens under mixed workloads
- bounding prefill queue depth / admission to avoid long prefill bursts starving decode + collectives

The intent is NOT to "solve" TP hangs, but to reduce incidence while deeper fixes roll through canary and to provide an easy on/off lever for SRE during incidents.

What changed
- New feature flag: runtime.tp.guardrails.v1
  - default: OFF globally
  - intended rollout: Dedicated canary clusters first (release plan tracked in ENG-4829)
- When enabled for TP replicas, apply guardrail defaults unless explicitly overridden by cluster config:
  - max_decode_concurrency_per_replica: 2 (was effectively unbounded aside from global worker limits)
  - max_inflight_prefills_per_replica: 1 (new; prevents prefill pileups)
  - max_prefill_batch_tokens: 16384 (new; tuned for A100/H100 mixed prompt distributions)

title: Load-shed new requests when TP stall detector trips (degrade replica + redirect/reject)
preview: Problem: When a TP replica is wedged (collective stall / scheduler starvation / heartbeat gap), we currently keep routing new work to it until upstream timeouts/backpressure eventually trigger. This amplifies p99, increases queue depth, and can cascade into autoscaling thrash. This PR wires the TP stall signal into overload protection so that once a replica is suspected-stalled it is immediately marked DEGRADING and stops accepting new requests; callers either get a fast, structured overload response or (when possible) are redirected to healthy replicas. Approach: 1) Introduce a "replica degraded" state driven by TP stall detector signals (from ENG-4823) with hysteresis to avoid flapping. 2) Teach the local admission controller + router to consult degraded state and load-shed before enqueue. 3) Surface a structured error code for clients and for internal retry logic, and export metrics so SRE can alert on load-shed events and correlate to heartbeat gaps. Behavior: - If tp_stall_detector trips for a replica (stall_reason != none) OR heartbeat gap exceeds threshold, set replica_state=DEGRADED for min 30s, then transition to RECOVERING once heartbeat resumes for a stable window. - While DEGRADED, admission controller rejects new requests with error_code=RUNTIME_TP_REPLICA_DEGRADED (HTTP 503 for hosted gateway, internal status for Dedicated). - Router: if other replicas for the same model+profile are healthy, redirect (internal reroute) before rejecting; otherwise fail fast to avoid unbounded queueing. - Existing in-flight requests are not force-cancelled by this change (watchdog/abort in ENG-4826 handles that path); this change only prevents pile-on. Metrics/logging: - runtime_tp_replica_degraded (gauge, labels: model, tp_degree, reason) - runtime_tp_load_shed_requests_total (counter, labels: reason, route, model) - Structured log at state transitions with replica_id, rank, reason, heartbeat_age_ms. Risk/mitigation: Primary risk is false positives causing unnecessary shedding. Mitigations: (a) hysteresis + minimum degraded window, (b) feature flag runtime.tp_load_shed_on_stall (default off in prod; enabled in canary), (c) metrics to quantify shed rate vs real stalls. Testing: Unit tests for state machine/hysteresis; integration test with simulated stall signal verifies that new requests are rejected/redirected while old requests continue (until watchdog in separate PR).

title: Runtime: TP watchdog timeout + safe abort/drain path for stalled steps
preview: Context: We have recurring Sev1/Sev2s where a TP replica silently stalls (most commonly during decode under mixed prefill/decode load). Today that manifests as requests hanging until upstream timeouts, with the replica continuing to accept work and compounding the blast radius. This PR introduces a feature-flagged watchdog that detects “no forward progress” in TP execution and triggers a controlled abort + replica drain. Goal is to fail fast, drain safely, and prevent GPU wedging / queue pileups while we continue to harden scheduler + NCCL ordering. What this PR does: (1) Adds a TP step watchdog that monitors progress at two levels: per-request “decode iteration progress” and per-rank “collective step counter” (fed by existing TP step bookkeeping; see ENG-4823/PR-21834 for richer metrics). The watchdog is implemented as a lightweight timer in the TP execution loop with a monotonic clock and is careful to avoid extra CUDA synchronizations in the healthy path. (2) Defines stall semantics and thresholds: a stall is declared when no progress is observed for longer than runtime.tp_watchdog.timeout_ms (default conservative; overridden via config by model class). We also support separate timeouts for prefill vs decode because long prefill can legitimately exceed decode thresholds on long-context prompts. (3) Adds safe abort behavior: on stall, we mark the replica “draining”, stop admitting new requests, and terminate in-flight TP work with a controlled error path. We intentionally do not attempt to recover the in-process TP state. Instead, we surface a structured RuntimeError with code TP_STALL_ABORTED, attach context (replica_id, tp_group_size, model_id, phase, last_progress_ms, pending_collectives), and rely on orchestrator/SRE automation to replace the replica. (4) Integrates SRE mitigation hooks: emits a drain event (rate-limited/deduped per replica) and a “stall detected” marker so SRE can tie alerts to automated drain/replace actions. This aligns with the on-call mitigation flow used in the 2025-02-17 incident (SUP-48321). (5) Feature flag + rollout: gated behind runtime.tp_watchdog.enabled (default off). Also supports runtime.tp_watchdog.drain_on_stall (default on when enabled) to allow dry-run in dev/perf environments where we only log. Implementation notes: - Watchdog avoids adding additional stream syncs; we only read host-side progress counters updated at known safe points. - Abort path cancels request futures, transitions scheduler to “reject new”, and triggers replica drain callback. - For streaming requests, we send a terminal error frame and close the stream to avoid client hangs. - Includes unit tests for stall detection and integration tests using a simulated “stalled step” hook (fault injection) rather than requiring real NCCL deadlocks in CI. Rollout/operational guidance: - Enable first in perf-canary with drain_on_stall=false to validate signal quality (no false positives). - Then enable drain_on_stall=true for a small Dedicated canary slice with extended logging (INT-2219) and strict rollback criteria (any increase in abort rate without correlated timeout reduction). - If we see false positives, raise per-model thresholds and/or narrow stall detection to decode-only while we refine progress accounting. Related: ENG-4826 (this change), ENG-4821 umbrella, PR-21834 (TP heartbeat/metrics), PR-21912 (error surfacing improvements; follow-up), PR-21875 (load-shed on stall signal; follow-up).

title: Add TP stall fault-injection tests to validate watchdog + abort semantics
preview:
Context: We’ve been hardening TP watchdog + abort/drain semantics (ADR-0127, ENG-4826) and adding stall detectors/metrics (ENG-4823). We didn’t have regression coverage that proves: (1) a rank/collective “silent stall” actually triggers the watchdog within the configured threshold, (2) the abort path returns the expected structured error, and (3) the replica drain/overload hooks fire so we don’t wedge GPUs indefinitely.

This PR adds a small fault-injection surface and an integration test suite that runs a multi-rank TP replica in-process (test harness spawns N ranks) and intentionally introduces controlled stalls. The goal is deterministic coverage without relying on real NCCL deadlocks.

What’s included:
- Fault injection knobs (test-only) to simulate the major stall classes we see in the wild:
  - "collective_never_completes": rank 0 blocks before a collective and never reaches the call site (simulates ordering mismatch/starvation without hanging the driver).
  - "collective_slow_path": inject an artificial delay before/after ncclGroupEnd to validate watchdog thresholds and false-positive behavior.
  - "scheduler_starvation": pause the TP collective scheduling lane while prefill work continues (validates fairness/guardrail integration).
  - "client_cancel_mid_decode": triggers cancel path during streaming decode to ensure watchdog/abort doesn’t leak in-flight state.
- New tests asserting the end-to-end semantics:
  - Watchdog fires and returns a stable error code (RuntimeError::kTpStallWatchdog) within bounds.
  - Abort propagates as a request-scoped failure and does not block unrelated replicas.
  - Replica drain is emitted once (deduped) and surfaces in logs/metrics.
  - Stall reason tagging is present in structured logs (compute vs comms vs scheduler) and increments the appropriate counters.
- CI reliability notes + gating behavior:
  - The suite is GPU-required and runs only on CI pools with multi-GPU nodes.
  - The “never completes” test uses a cooperative stall (condition variable + timeout) rather than blocking CUDA/NCCL, so the test process can still exit cleanly.
  - Default runtime watchdog thresholds are overridden in tests to keep runtime < 60s total.

Why this is safe:
- Fault injection is guarded behind a build flag and a runtime check: enabled only for tests (RW_ENABLE_FAULT_INJECTION=1) and only in non-release builds.
- No behavior changes in production builds.

How to run locally:
- bazel test //runtime/tp/tests:tp_watchdog_fault_injection_test --config=cuda
- RW_ENABLE_FAULT_INJECTION=1 RW_TP_WATCHDOG_TIMEOUT_MS=8000 bazel test //runtime/tp/tests:tp_watchdog_fault_injection_test

Follow-ups:
- Once perf-canary has the TP stress scenarios live (ENG-4828), we should add a “simulated stall canary” run in benchmark-lab to validate alert routing end-to-end (SRE).

Checklist:
- [x] Deterministic repro of watchdog firing
- [x] Verifies structured error propagation to API boundary (via internal request wrapper)
- [x] Ensures replica drain hook called exactly once
- [x] CI configured to avoid flakiness / hangs
- [x] Docs/comments added near injection points explaining constraints

Refs: ENG-4826 (watchdog + drain), ENG-4823 (stall metrics/reasons), INT-2219 (extra debug logging window during canary).

title: Dedicated: tier-aware admission control guards (queue caps + overload shedding)
preview:
Context

Dedicated throughput guarantees require us to keep tail latency and per-deployment throughput stable even when a pool is oversubscribed (mis-sized customers, sudden bursts, noisy neighbors, or control-plane misroutes). Today, overload behavior is mostly “best effort” and can lead to queue buildup that drags the entire pool down.

This PR implements tier-aware admission control guards in the gateway->orchestrator path:
- queue caps per (deployment_id, route) with tier-specific ceilings
- overload shedding rules that preferentially shed lower-tier/burst traffic while protecting committed Dedicated throughput floors
- consistent, observable rejection semantics (429 vs 503) and reason codes

Links
- Linear: ENG-19257 (Tier-aware admission control)
- Related: ENG-19244 / PR-19321 (per-deployment throughput metrics), ENG-19263 / PR-19366 (tier->pool mapping)
- Support signal: SUP-18422 (customer-reported throughput below commitment during saturation)

What changed

1) Admission control policy (tier-aware)
- Introduced AdmissionPolicyEvaluator with inputs: tier, workload class (dedicated_committed vs dedicated_burst), route_type (chat/embeddings/rerank), and pool saturation signals.
- Default queue caps (configurable per region/pool):
  - Standard: smaller per-deployment queue cap, more aggressive shedding when pool_saturation > threshold
  - Plus: medium queue cap, protected longer before shedding

text:
Context

Dedicated throughput guarantees require us to keep tail latency and per-deployment throughput stable even when a pool is oversubscribed (mis-sized customers, sudden bursts, noisy neighbors, or control-plane misroutes). Today, overload behavior is mostly “best effort” and can lead to queue buildup that drags the entire pool down.

- queue caps per (deployment_id, route) with tier-specific ceilings

title: TP: harden NCCL collective stream/event synchronization (graph-safe, feature-flagged)
preview:
Context: We have recurring TP hangs under sustained mixed prefill/decode load where one or more ranks appear to block indefinitely in/around NCCL collectives. In incident traces and NCCL debug logs (see SUP-48377), we consistently see patterns consistent with stream ordering/synchronization assumptions being violated across compute/comm streams when prefill and decode interleave and when CUDA Graph capture is enabled for steady-state decode. This PR adds explicit stream/event synchronization around NCCL collectives to make the ordering guarantees deterministic and to prevent silent stalls caused by missing dependencies.

What this changes: 1) Introduces a dedicated NCCL stream per TP replica (existing) but enforces a consistent dependency contract: compute -> (record event) -> NCCL stream waits -> launch collective -> (record event) -> compute stream waits. 2) Adds a small helper (NcclSyncGuard) used at all collective callsites (all-reduce, all-gather, reduce-scatter) to avoid ad-hoc event wiring and ensure we do not accidentally skip a wait under certain scheduler paths. 3) Makes behavior conditional for CUDA Graph capture: during capture, we avoid calling cudaEventRecord/cudaStreamWaitEvent in ways that break capture or introduce illegal dependencies. Instead, we (a) pre-create capture-safe events and (b) fall back to a conservative single-stream path for collectives in graph mode when the runtime determines the capture would otherwise include cross-stream dependencies. 4) Adds structured debug logging (rate-limited) when the sync guard takes the conservative path so we can correlate any performance regressions to graph-mode fallbacks.

Why this fixes the hang class: NCCL collectives are launched on the NCCL stream, but upstream compute that produces input buffers and downstream compute that consumes outputs were occasionally scheduled on different streams without a strict event edge. Under load, the scheduler can reorder work such that a rank enters a collective with incomplete inputs or a different collective ordering, producing stalls that look like "NCCL waiting" with no explicit error. The explicit event edges ensure every rank sees the same dependency ordering from compute to NCCL and back.

Perf considerations: This adds two events and two waits per collective in the non-graph path. Microbench results on A100-80GB show +0.4–0.9% median decode latency overhead at 4-way TP for typical batch sizes; p99 impact in stress runs was within noise once stalls were eliminated. In graph mode, we prefer keeping capture intact; if we must fall back to a conservative path, we log it and increment a counter so we can quantify how often we lose graph benefits.

Rollout plan: Feature-flagged behind runtime.tp.nccl_sync_hardening (default ON in canary, OFF in general until validated). SRE requested a guard to disable quickly if perf regressions show up; this is wired to the dynamic config system and also supports env override REDWOOD_TP_NCCL_SYNC_HARDENING=0 for emergency rollback.

Testing: Added unit coverage for dependency wiring (mock stream/event API) and an integration smoke test that runs a short multi-rank decode loop with forced stream interleaving. Also validated with benchmark-lab repro harness and the mixed prefill/decode scenario from ENG-4822 (local).

Snippet (illustrative): In the collective wrapper we now do: compute_event.record(compute_stream); nccl_stream.wait(compute_event); ncclAllReduce(..., nccl_stream); nccl_done.record(nccl_stream); compute_stream.wait(nccl_done). Graph mode uses capture-safe event objects and may switch to a single stream if cross-stream waits would invalidate capture.

Follow-ups: We still need better surfacing when NCCL stalls (ENG-4823/PR-21872) and a watchdog/abort path (ENG-4826). This PR is intentionally focused on hardening the ordering contract at the kernel/comms boundary (ENG-4825).

title: Add Private backup/restore dashboards + alert rules (backup success, duration, restore drill outcomes)
preview: Context: As part of the Private Day-2 backup/restore toolkit, we need an opinionated observability pack that SRE/Solutions can deploy into customer VPC/on-prem environments to answer two questions quickly: (1) are backups happening and completing successfully on schedule, and (2) are restore drills passing and meeting RTO targets in staging. This PR adds a set of dashboards and alert rules designed to work across hosted VPC installs and constrained on-prem/air-gapped setups (where available metrics are limited and log shipping may be disabled). Scope: (A) dashboards for backup job success rate, last_success_ts, duration, bytes, and error categories; (B) dashboards for restore drill outcomes (pass/fail, duration, validation phase breakdown) and drill cadence; (C) alert rules with conservative defaults plus documented tuning for noisy environments (slow object storage, intermittent KMS throttling, planned maintenance). Metrics assumed: redwood_private_backup_job_success_total, redwood_private_backup_job_duration_seconds, redwood_private_backup_bytes_total, redwood_private_backup_last_success_timestamp_seconds, redwood_private_restore_drill_success_total, redwood_private_restore_drill_duration_seconds, redwood_private_restore_validation_phase_duration_seconds, and redwood_private_backup_error_total{category=...} (emitted by control-plane + installer hooks in related PRs). Dashboards/alerts are grouped under a new folder 'Redwood Private / Backup & DR'. Tuning notes: alert thresholds use per-install labels (deployment_id, environment, cluster) and are designed to avoid paging during planned maintenance by supporting a maintenance label/recording rule override; duration alerts are based on p95 over a 6h window to reduce sensitivity to occasional NFS/S3 jitter; stale backup alert uses last_success_ts with configurable expected_interval (default 24h) and grace window (default +6h). Out of scope: defining the core metrics emission (handled in redwood monorepo), paging policy selection (SRE runbook), and customer-specific routing/notification integrations. Validation: Tested in staging with a simulated failing backup job and an intentionally broken restore drill; ensured alerts fire and resolve; verified dashboard variables work when some metric series are absent (air-gapped mode without bytes_total). Screenshots are attached in the PR comments (not committed).

title: Improve NCCL error surfacing to API layer (actionable error codes + routing hints)
preview:
Context: Under TP load, we’ve had multiple incidents/support escalations where replicas wedge in a NCCL collective (or watchdog aborts the step), but the request surfaces upstream as a generic deadline exceeded / internal error. That prevents (a) smart routing/fallback from kicking in quickly, (b) customers from getting actionable diagnostics, and (c) on-call from correlating API timeouts to a specific NCCL failure mode. This PR wires the runtime’s NCCL/TP failure signals (including watchdog-triggered aborts) through the internal RPC boundary to the API layer and normalizes them into stable error codes + routing hints.

What changed: 1) Introduced stable RuntimeErrorCode values for TP/NCCL failure modes (kept intentionally small + versionable): RWD_TP_NCCL_TIMEOUT, RWD_TP_NCCL_ABORTED, RWD_TP_NCCL_COLLECTIVE_MISMATCH, RWD_TP_NCCL_UNHANDLED, RWD_TP_REPLICA_DRAINED. 2) Runtime now captures NCCL failures at the comms boundary and wraps them as a typed RuntimeError (instead of a generic Status::Internal) with safe context fields: tp_rank, tp_world_size, collective_seq (monotonic), last_collective (enum), watchdog_fired (bool), and a redacted nccl_error_string (guarded behind existing debug flag; default off in Hosted). 3) API gateway maps RuntimeErrorCode -> customer error response with consistent shape and HTTP status: - Timeout/Abort/Mismatch: HTTP 503 (service unavailable) with error.type="runtime_stall" and error.code set to the stable code. - Replica drained: HTTP 503 with error.type="replica_unavailable" and retry_after_ms populated when known. 4) Added RoutingHint to internal response metadata: retryable (bool), fallback_allowed (bool), and preferred_action (RETRY_SAME_MODEL | FALLBACK_COMPAT_MODEL | REROUTE_REGION). Initial policy: NCCL_TIMEOUT / NCCL_ABORTED => retryable=true, fallback_allowed=true; COLLECTIVE_MISMATCH => retryable=false (to avoid hot-looping) but fallback_allowed=true; REPLICA_DRAINED => retryable=true. 5) Streaming path: ensure we terminate the stream with the structured error payload (previously some paths hung until client timeout when the backend died mid-stream). We now emit a final error frame and close the stream.

Why this is safe: - Codes are derived from a narrow set of runtime conditions we already detect (NCCL error return, watchdog abort reason). - Context fields are intentionally limited and do not include prompts/outputs; rank/world_size/collective sequence are non-sensitive. - Customer-visible strings are generic; detailed NCCL strings remain behind the existing diagnostic flag and are intended for controlled canary windows (see INT-2219).

Illustrative payload: {"error": {"type": "runtime_stall", "code": "RWD_TP_NCCL_ABORTED", "message": "Tensor-parallel execution aborted due to a stalled collective.", "retryable": true, "fallback_allowed": true, "request_id": "..."}}

Files/areas touched (high level): - runtime/comms/nccl_wrapper.*: map NCCL return values + watchdog abort reason -> RuntimeErrorCode - runtime/tp/executor.*: plumb typed errors through TP step boundaries (prefill/decode) - rpc/runtime_service.proto + generated clients: add RuntimeError + RoutingHint to response envelope - gateway/api_error_mapping.*: normalize to customer payload + http status - streaming/bridge.*: ensure mid-stream backend failure yields a final structured error frame - tracing/spans.*: attach error.code and (when debug enabled) tp_rank/collective_seq tags

Test plan: - Unit tests: mapping coverage for NCCL errors and watchdog abort reasons; API error mapping snapshot tests. - Integration tests: simulated NCCL abort injected in TP executor -> API response contains RWD_TP_NCCL_ABORTED and retryable=true; streaming test verifies final error frame and stream closure. - Manual: ran benchmark-lab repro harness (mixed prefill/decode) with injected abort path; confirmed Dedicated canary correctly falls back to compatible single-GPU model when enabled.

Rollout notes: - No behavior change unless a NCCL/TP failure occurs; normal success path unaffected. - RoutingHint consumption is gated behind an existing runtime routing feature flag (on for Dedicated canary only per ENG-4829). - Follow-up: dashboard/alerts will key off these codes (tracked in observability pack PR-412).

Checklist: - [x] Added stable error codes and docs in code comments - [x] Ensured no customer payload includes sensitive model inputs/outputs - [x] Added tests for streaming + non-streaming - [x] Validated with SRE (paging/alertability) and Support (actionable customer messaging)

Links: Linear ENG-4826 (watchdog), ENG-4825 (NCCL hardening), ENG-4823 (instrumentation), Support SUP-48377. Incident context: 2025-02-17 Sev1 (TP decode hang).

title: Scheduler: guardrail to prevent TP collective starvation under prefill-heavy load
preview:
Context
We have a repeated hang/timeout signature in TP replicas under sustained mixed prefill/decode load where collectives become ready but never get scheduled promptly because the scheduler keeps selecting prefill-heavy work that maximizes token throughput. When one rank gets delayed on a collective boundary while others enter the collective path, NCCL ordering diverges and we end up in a silent stall (or downstream request timeouts).

This PR adds a narrow scheduler guardrail: when a TP group has collective-ready work pending beyond a bounded window, we temporarily bias scheduling decisions to ensure the collective boundary is reached and executed across ranks. The goal is correctness/stability first, with minimal perf impact.

What changed (high level)
- Introduce the concept of "collective-ready" work items in the runtime scheduler for TP replicas.
- Track a per-TP-group "collective_ready_age_ms" and "collective_ready_depth" (how many steps are waiting at a collective boundary).
- Add a fairness rule: if collective_ready_age_ms exceeds a threshold OR collective_ready_depth exceeds a small limit, we throttle selecting additional prefill-heavy batches and instead drain collective-ready items until the TP group is back in a healthy state.
- Ensure the policy is deterministic across ranks by basing the decision on rank-0 broadcast state (same inputs => same ordering decisions).
- Add metrics + structured logs to make this visible in production and in repro harnesses.

Why this is safe
- The guardrail is behind a feature flag: `runtime.scheduler.tp_collective_fairness_guardrail`.
- Default behavior remains unchanged unless enabled.
- The policy only activates when we are already in a high-risk state (collective-ready work aging / queueing).

Implementation details
- New scheduler hook `TpCollectiveFairnessController` evaluates TP group health on each tick.
- Added a bounded "prefill_budget" when fairness is active. Prefill work can still proceed, but it cannot indefinitely starve collective-ready work.
- Added a deterministic state propagation: rank 0 computes `fairness_mode` and the current budget, then shares it as part of the TP control-plane message. Non-zero ranks treat the state as authoritative.
- Added a small jitter tolerance to avoid flapping when workloads hover around the threshold.

Perf comparison (benchmark-lab)
Environment
- HW: A100 80GB, TP=4, 1 replica unless specified
- Models: 70B-class decoder-only (internal build; see benchmark sheet link in ENG-4824)
- Workload: mixed prefill/decode with long tail prompt lengths, streaming enabled

Key results (median of 5 runs; higher is better for throughput, lower is better for latency)
1) Mixed workload (prefill-heavy, high concurrency)
- Baseline (flag off): p50 65 ms/token, p99 310 ms/token, throughput 1.00x, hang repro rate 2/5 runs
- Guardrail (flag on): p50 67 ms/token (+3.1%), p99 235 ms/token (-24.2%), throughput 0.98x (-2.0%), hang repro rate 0/5 runs

2) Decode-biased workload (short prompts, steady decode)
- Baseline: throughput 1.00x, p99 stable
- Guardrail: throughput 1.00x (no measurable change), fairness activation ~0 (as expected)

3) Worst-case long-context bursts (burst concurrency + long prompts)
- Baseline: occasional large p99 spikes and one stall in 3 runs
- Guardrail: p99 spikes reduced; no stalls in 3 runs; fairness activation correlated with burst windows

Notes:
- The p50 regression is expected when the guardrail activates because we stop greedily stuffing prefill. Given the stability gain and p99 improvement, this tradeoff is acceptable for Dedicated canary. We can tune thresholds per model/hardware after we have more production data.

Metrics added
- `runtime_scheduler_tp_collective_ready_age_ms{tp_group_id,model,cluster}` (histogram)
- `runtime_scheduler_tp_fairness_mode_active{tp_group_id}` (gauge)
- `runtime_scheduler_tp_prefill_throttle_total{reason}` (counter; reason = collective_age|collective_depth)
- Log: `tp_fairness_decision` structured event (includes budgets and queue summary; sampled)

Testing
- Unit tests for fairness controller state machine and determinism across ranks.
- Integration test using the TP hang repro harness config (ENG-4822) updated to assert fairness-mode triggers and that the scheduler drains collective-ready items within bounded time.

Rollout plan
- Land behind feature flag (this PR).
- Enable in perf-canary TP stress scenarios once ENG-4828 thresholds are updated.
- Dedicated-first canary for affected accounts (see PM-911 + SUP-48344) before broad Hosted enablement.

Follow-ups (not in this PR)
- Make thresholds dynamic based on observed seq length distribution (could reduce p50 impact).
- Add a "stall reason" correlation in tracing spans (ENG-4823 / PR-21872).

Refs
- Linear: ENG-4824 (scheduler ordering/fairness), ENG-4821 (initiative)
- Support: SUP-48344 (streaming timeouts during peak load), SUP-48321 (Dedicated A100 hangs)
- Related PRs: PR-21834 (heartbeat metrics), PR-21853 (NCCL stream sync hardening)

Checklist
- [x] Feature-flagged
- [x] Metrics added + documented in code comments
- [x] Unit + integration coverage
- [x] Benchmark results attached in ENG-4824
- [x] Rollback: disable flag (no migration)

title: Fix deadlock on cancelled streaming requests in tensor-parallel decode
preview:
Context: We’ve observed intermittent hangs/timeouts on TP replicas correlated with streaming responses during peak load. One reproducible signature is a client disconnect (or server-side cancellation) mid-decode that triggers an early-exit path on one rank while other ranks continue into the next NCCL collective. Because our TP decode loop assumes all ranks execute an identical sequence of collectives per step, this can leave some ranks waiting indefinitely in ncclGroupEnd()/allReduce while another rank has already unwound. This PR fixes the cancellation path to preserve collective ordering invariants across ranks and to fail fast rather than wedging the replica.

Repro: Added an integration repro that opens a streaming request, reads N chunks, then hard-closes the client connection (RST) and repeats under concurrency against a TP=4 replica with mixed prefill/decode. Prior to this change the replica can wedge with no explicit NCCL error, while request-level timeouts climb and heartbeats stop.

Fix overview: (1) Introduce a per-step TP cancellation handshake: when any rank observes request cancellation, it sets a local abort flag and participates in a small control collective (allReduce OR on abort) at a defined synchronization point before the next compute/collective boundary. (2) Ensure we do not return early from the per-rank decode loop until we have either (a) completed the step’s required collectives or (b) performed the abort handshake and transitioned into a uniform abort path that all ranks execute. (3) On abort, mark the request as cancelled with a structured runtime error code and drain the replica if we detect we were mid-step (this aligns with ENG-4826 semantics; draining is guarded and rate-limited).

Implementation details: - Adds TPAbortState tracked per in-flight request group (request_id + tp_group_id). - Adds explicit ‘cancel_sync’ barrier in the decode step state machine to avoid skipping collectives. - Moves client-disconnect cancellation handling out of the per-token stream writer and into the request lifecycle so it’s visible to the scheduler/TP driver in time. - Ensures CUDA graph capture mode bypasses cancel_sync (graphs require fixed control flow); in graph mode we instead defer cancellation until a safe boundary and return a CANCELLED status without early-exit. - Improves logs/metrics: emits tp_abort_reason=client_disconnect|server_cancel|deadline_exceeded and includes rank, step, and in_flight_collectives counter.

Testing: - New regression test that simulates a client disconnect while streaming (uses a test client that sends RST) and asserts the replica continues to serve subsequent requests without wedging. - Stress test variant that runs cancellation under concurrency with long-context + streaming decode. - Added a fault-injection unit test for the decode step state machine to validate uniform abort path is taken across ranks.

Risk/rollout: Small but safety-sensitive; modifies decode control flow. Change is behind runtime flag runtime.tp.cancel_sync=on by default for canary; we can flip off if we see perf regression (expected overhead ~1 tiny control collective only when cancellation happens; no impact to steady-state decode). If we observe any increase in false replica drains, we’ll tighten the ‘mid-step’ heuristic and/or route to load-shed instead.

References: ENG-4821 (initiative), ENG-4822 (repro harness), SUP-48344/SUP-48377 (customer reports), and the 2025-02-17 incident signature (client disconnect + streaming timeouts on TP replicas).

title: Add deploy context card (recent deploys + canary status) to incident thread diagnostics
preview: Motivation: During recent incidents we repeatedly lost time answering “what changed?” in the first 5 minutes. This PR adds a new Incident Bot diagnostics card that attaches deploy context directly into the incident thread, including recent deploys for the affected service/region, rollout phase, canary health, and commit/image identifiers. It’s designed to be tenant-safe and useful even when deeper telemetry is timing out. Scope: (1) New Slack “Recent deploys” context card rendered in the thread reply alongside existing diagnostics cards. (2) Backend integration to fetch recent deploy metadata from the release registry (service -> last N deploy events) and normalize it into the bot’s context payload. (3) Links out to the deploy timeline view and the referenced commit/PR when available. UX notes: The card summarizes up to 3 most recent deploys in the last 6 hours, then provides a “View full deploy timeline” link. Canary state is highlighted (PASS/FAIL/UNKNOWN) with the relevant % traffic and rollout step when present; missing fields degrade gracefully. Implementation details: Introduces a DeployContextCollector that calls release-metadata-service with a strict timeout (800ms) and returns partial results on timeout. Slack blocks are generated via a new deploy_context.ts renderer with truncation rules to avoid overly verbose output in #incidents. Adds unit tests for the render output and integration tests that validate we never show more than the allowed number of rows and we don’t fail the entire diagnostics payload if the release service is unavailable. Follow-ups: Feature flagging/cohort rollout is tracked separately; this PR is wiring + rendering only (it can be toggled via the existing diagnostics.enable_deploy_context config knob in dev/staging).

title: router: apply route experiment selection before fallback policy evaluation
preview: Problem: When a route has both (a) a fallback policy (e.g., model fallback / region fallback) and (b) a route experiment (A/B variants), the router currently evaluates fallbacks first and only then attempts experiment selection. In practice that means experiments can be silently bypassed during mild capacity pressure or transient model health flips, and worse, attribution becomes misleading because traffic that should be split is instead routed via fallback and never hits the intended variant. This PR changes the routing order so experiment selection happens before fallback evaluation, while preserving safety behavior when experiment config is missing/invalid/unavailable. New order (high-level): 1) Resolve route + canonical route_id; 2) Evaluate eligibility + deterministic assignment for the experiment (if enabled and applicable) to pick a target variant; 3) Evaluate fallback policy for the selected variant target (model/region/tier) to ensure we still land on a healthy/available endpoint; 4) If fallback results in a different model/region than requested by variant, emit explicit metrics + debug fields so the results pipeline can reason about ‘variant overridden by fallback’. Safety/default behavior: If experiment evaluation fails closed (config fetch error, config not yet propagated, invalid variant configuration, missing stickiness key when required by policy), we fall back to the existing route default selection path and then evaluate fallback as before (i.e., no new failure mode for request routing). This is also gated behind the existing route-experiments feature flag in the router config so we can roll out gradually. Metrics added: router.route_experiments.pre_fallback_selected_total (count of requests where experiment assignment occurred prior to fallback), router.route_experiments.fallback_overrode_variant_total (count of requests where assignment happened but fallback changed the chosen target), router.route_experiments.assignment_error_total (assignment attempted but failed, labeled by error_type), and router.route_experiments.evaluator_latency_ms (timing for experiment evaluation). These metrics are emitted with low-cardinality tags (route_id, experiment_id, variant_id where present; region and model_id only on sampled spans per existing OTel conventions). Logging/attribution: The request context now retains both ‘assigned_variant’ and ‘final_routed_target’ so downstream telemetry can distinguish ‘variant traffic’ vs ‘variant selected but overridden’. This PR does not change the attribution schema itself (ADR-048); it ensures the router produces consistent assignment decisions earlier in the decision graph. Testing: Added unit tests covering precedence behavior and ensured existing fallback tests still pass. Added an integration test harness case that simulates a degraded primary model for one variant and validates that fallback occurs after assignment (and increments fallback_overrode_variant_total). Rollback plan: (1) flip router feature flag off to restore previous evaluation order without redeploy; (2) if needed, revert commit (safe: no persisted schema changes); (3) monitor SLO guardrails: error rate, p99 routing latency, and fallback_overrode_variant_total spike as a sign of misconfigured variants. Notes: This matches the decision captured in the routing/rollouts precedence discussion (apply experiment first, then allow fallback to preserve availability). This unblocks Console from showing meaningful ‘variant health’ even when fallbacks are active, because we can now measure how often a variant is being overridden rather than silently skipping the experiment.

title: Backpressure controls + max lag safety (auto-pause) for downstream outages
preview:
Context: We had a beta incident where Splunk HEC throttling (429 + intermittent 503) caused exporter lag to spike and sustained retries increased queue pressure and CPU, impacting other tenants sharing the same exporter shard (see #incidents thread 1739559010 and SUP-18531 follow-up). This PR introduces explicit backpressure controls and a max-lag safety mechanism to keep the system stable during downstream outages while preserving at-least-once semantics.

What’s in this PR: (1) Max lag threshold per destination with auto-pause: if export_lag_seconds for a (tenant, destination) exceeds configurable max_lag_seconds for a sustained window, we stop pulling new batches for that destination and mark it PAUSED (reason=MAX_LAG) while continuing to process other destinations/tenants. (2) Backpressure based on in-flight bytes and retry backlog: introduce per-destination in_flight_bytes limits and a bounded retry queue; when exceeded we apply pull-side backoff rather than pushing more work into retries. (3) Circuit breaker integration for adapters: adapter-specific throttling signals (e.g., Splunk 429, Datadog 429/5xx) now feed a shared circuit breaker that increases backoff, and can trip into PAUSED when combined with lag growth. (4) Operator controls: new CLI commands to unpause a destination and to override max lag for controlled recovery. (5) Observability: new Prometheus metrics and structured log fields for pause/unpause transitions, reasons, and “time paused”; status endpoint now includes pause state and last_pause_reason.

Delivery semantics: No events are dropped. Auto-pause stops the exporter from advancing its checkpoint for the paused destination, so events remain available for replay/backfill once the downstream recovers and the destination is unpaused. This is consistent with our at-least-once contract; downstream dedup remains based on event_id.

Configuration: Adds exporter config keys (with safe defaults): max_lag_seconds (default 21600 / 6h), max_lag_grace_period_seconds (default 300), max_in_flight_bytes (default 64MiB), max_retry_queue_batches (default 500), pause_cooldown_seconds (default 900). All are feature-flagged behind BACKPRESSURE_V1 to limit beta blast radius.

Testing: unit tests for PauseController transitions; integration test with Splunk stub that returns sustained 429 and verifies: lag rises -> pause triggers -> pulling stops -> unpause resumes and drains; chaos-ish test ensures healthy destination continues despite one paused. Load test notes included in commit message, based on SRE backpressure experiments doc.

Rollout plan: Enable BACKPRESSURE_V1 for beta tenants only after deploying exporter + dashboards. Default max lag is conservative; SRE can tune per-tenant via config overrides if needed.

Checklist: [x] Metrics + dashboards hooks (exporter_paused, pause_reason, pause_duration_seconds) [x] Runbook updates TODO in separate PR (Sean requested) [x] Security review: no new PII; logs redact destination secrets [x] Feature flag gated (BACKPRESSURE_V1) [x] Integration tests cover 429/5xx.

Follow-ups (not in this PR): (a) surface pause/unpause controls in Console (control plane) (b) alerting thresholds tuning once we have 1-2 weeks of beta data (c) doc updates for customer-facing explanation of auto-pause + recovery expectations.

(3) Circuit breaker integration for adapters: adapter-specific throttling signals (e.g., Splunk 429, Datadog 429/5xx) now feed a shared circuit breaker that increases backoff, and can trip into PAUSED when combined with lag growth. (4) Operator controls: new CLI commands to unpause a destination and to override max lag for controlled recovery. (5) Observability: new Prometheus metrics and structured log fields for pause/unpause transitions, reasons, and “time paused”; status endpoint now includes pause state and last_pause_reason.

Delivery semantics: No events are dropped. Auto-pause stops the exporter from advancing its checkpoint for the paused destination, so events remain available for replay/backfill once the downstream recovers and the destination is unpaused. This is consistent with our at-least-once contract; downstream dedup remains based on event_id.

Configuration: Adds exporter config keys (with safe defaults): max_lag_seconds (default 21600 / 6h), max_lag_grace_period_seconds (default 300), max_in_flight_bytes (default 64MiB), max_retry_queue_batches (default 500), pause_cooldown_seconds (default 900). All are feature-flagged behind BACKPRESSURE_V1 to limit beta blast radius.

Testing: unit tests for PauseController transitions; integration test with Splunk stub that returns sustained 429 and verifies: lag rises -> pause triggers -> pulling stops -> unpause resumes and drains; chaos-ish test ensures healthy destination continues despite one paused. Load test notes included in commit message, based on SRE backpressure experiments doc.

Rollout plan: Enable BACKPRESSURE_V1 for beta tenants only after deploying exporter + dashboards. Default max lag is conservative; SRE can tune per-tenant via config overrides if needed.

Checklist: [x] Metrics + dashboards hooks (exporter_paused, pause_reason, pause_duration_seconds) [x] Runbook updates TODO in separate PR (Sean requested) [x] Security review: no new PII; logs redact destination secrets [x] Feature flag gated (BACKPRESSURE_V1) [x] Integration tests cover 429/5xx.

Follow-ups (not in this PR): (a) surface pause/unpause controls in Console (control plane) (b) alerting thresholds tuning once we have 1-2 weeks of beta data (c) doc updates for customer-facing explanation of auto-pause + recovery expectations.

title: Add dashboards + alerts for data residency enforcement (blocked requests, policy eval latency)
preview: Summary: This PR adds a first-pass set of dashboards and alerting rules for Hosted data residency enforcement. The focus is on (1) visibility into enforcement outcomes (allowed vs blocked), (2) early detection of regressions in policy evaluation latency, and (3) detecting cross-region routing attempts that should be prevented once enforcement is enabled. Context: As part of Hosted data residency enforcement rollout (region pinning + deterministic request-time blocking), SRE and Security asked for a consistent set of metrics visualizations and actionable alerts. This pack is intended to be deployed alongside the routing/gateway changes and used during dogfood and progressive rollout. What’s included: Grafana dashboard: “Data Residency Enforcement” with panels: Overview: total requests, allowed/blocked, block-rate (%), and top block reasons (5m/1h). Blocked requests by region + endpoint: breakdown by edge region, model route, and request_type (streaming vs non-streaming). Blocked requests by org/project (top N): uses org_id/project_id labels when present; falls back to org_tier if IDs are intentionally redacted in some environments. Policy evaluation latency: p50/p95/p99 over time from residency policy decision histogram. Routing attempts: cross-region routing attempt counter and fallback suppression counter (to validate that fallback is being blocked unless explicitly allowed). Error contract adoption: panel showing residency_violation responses vs generic 5xx on streaming path (to catch the SUP-28577 class of issues). Alerts: 1) residency.block_rate_high (severity=page): triggers when blocked requests spike above expected baseline after filtering out known test orgs. Default threshold: > 2% block-rate AND > 30 blocked requests/min for 10m, grouped by region. 2) residency.block_rate_high_ticket (severity=ticket): triggers at lower thresholds to create early-warning noise without paging. Default threshold: > 0.5% AND > 10/min for 15m. 3) residency.policy_eval_latency_p95 (severity=page): p95 residency decision latency > 25ms for 10m, grouped by region (catches cache misses, policy service slowness, or routing layer regressions). 4) residency.cross_region_attempts (severity=page): any sustained non-zero cross-region routing attempt rate (> 1/min for 10m) after enforcement is enabled (gated via a deployment label so we don’t page during pre-enforcement dry runs). Example queries (PromQL) used in panels/alerts: Block rate (region): sum(rate(redwood_residency_enforcement_decision_total{decision="blocked"}[5m])) by (region) / clamp_min(sum(rate(redwood_residency_enforcement_decision_total[5m])) by (region), 1) Top block reasons: topk(10, sum(rate(redwood_residency_enforcement_decision_total{decision="blocked"}[15m])) by (block_reason)) Policy eval p95 (region): histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(redwood_residency_policy_eval_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, region)) Cross-region attempts: sum(rate(redwood_residency_cross_region_routing_attempt_total[5m])) by (src_region, dst_region) Notes/assumptions: Metric names match the current instrumentation plan in routing/gateway branches: redwood_residency_enforcement_decision_total (labels: decision, block_reason, region, endpoint, request_type, org_tier, org_id?, project_id?, policy_id?) redwood_residency_policy_eval_duration_seconds_bucket redwood_residency_cross_region_routing_attempt_total redwood_residency_fallback_suppressed_total If labels diverge slightly in final implementation, we can follow up with a small patch; this PR keeps queries isolated in jsonnet so updates are straightforward. Files changed (high level): dashboards/residency/enforcement-overview.jsonnet (new) dashboards/residency/enforcement-drilldowns.jsonnet (new) dashboards/residency/enforcement-errors.jsonnet (new) alerts/residency-enforcement.rules.yml (new) mixins/residency/README.md (new runbook links + definitions) dashboards/_index.jsonnet (register new dashboard) alerts/_index.yml (register new rules) Review notes: Sean Gallagher (SRE): Requested separating page vs ticket alerts and adding minimum volume gates to avoid paging during low-traffic regions. Addressed by adding dual alerts and clamp/volume checks; also added a panel for raw blocked count next to percentage. Rafael Mendes (SRE Manager): Asked for explicit links to runbooks and to tag alerts with the rollout phase label so we can disable paging during Phase 2 staging validation. Added runbook URLs and a required label residency_enforcement_enabled="true" to paging alerts where applicable. Logan Wright (Telemetry/audit): Confirmed that org_id/project_id labels are acceptable in internal metrics; requested a guard to allow redaction in some environments. Implemented by building panels that work with org_tier and optional org_id/project_id. Mina Haddad (Routing): Verified cross-region attempt panels align with how routing will emit src/dst labels; requested a panel to show fallback suppressed vs fallback attempted. Added a small comparison panel. Testing/verification: Rendered dashboards via jsonnet build locally; validated against staging Prometheus with synthetic counters from the residency canary job. Confirmed alert rules load successfully and evaluate with expected firing behavior when replaying a spike scenario. CI: lint-jsonnet: pass prometheus-rules-validate: pass dashboard-render-smoke: pass Outcome: Merged (squash) after approvals; safe to deploy with the next observability-pack rollout. Follow-ups: (1) Once gateway error contract is fully rolled out on streaming endpoints, we should tighten the “generic 5xx on streaming” panel and convert it into an alert for regressions (tracked in ENG-4833). (2) Consider adding an SLO panel (block-rate budget) per allowlisted enterprise org during Phase 3 rollout.

title: Add ExperimentConfig CRD + server-side validation for route experiments
preview: Context This PR introduces the initial ExperimentConfig schema (CRD-style config object) used by the control plane + policy engine to define route-level experiments (A/B/n). It includes strict server-side validation and user-facing error messages that Console can surface directly. This is the first building block for deterministic bucketing as defined in ADR-047. Links Linear: ENG-9021 ADR: ADR-047-deterministic-experiment-bucketing What’s included Schema / model Introduces ExperimentConfig with: metadata: experiment_id, display_name, owner_team, created_by, description (optional) scope: account_id (tenant), environment, route_id(s) variants: variant_id, name, allocation (0..1), target (model_id + optional runtime overrides), enabled flag (default true) assignment: stickiness_key (enum), hash_seed, fallback_key_behavior, allow_missing_identifiers flag guardrails: optional thresholds (p95/p99 latency delta, error rate delta, cost/token delta) as placeholders for later wiring (not evaluating yet) state: draft|running|stopping|stopped (stored, but state transitions are handled in the experiment service PRs) Validation rules (server-side) High-level: fail closed, deterministic, and avoid ambiguous routing behavior. Specific checks implemented: Required fields: experiment_id, at least one route_id, >= 2 variants, and assignment.stickiness_key must be set (no implicit default yet). Allocation checks: allocation must be > 0 for enabled variants; sum of enabled variant allocations must be 1.0 (within epsilon 1e-6). If a variant is disabled, allocation must be 0.0. Variant limits: max 8 variants per experiment (guardrail to reduce config/routing complexity and metrics cardinality). Uniqueness: variant_id unique within experiment; route_id unique list; experiment_id unique within {account_id, environment}. Model compatibility validation (shallow): ensures referenced model_id exists in catalog for the environment and that it is eligible for the route’s modality (chat vs embeddings vs rerank). (Full compatibility checks will be enforced later by policy engine/routing integration.) Stickiness key constraints: only allow keys that are stable and cross-region deterministic (per ADR-047). Valid options in this PR: customer_id, end_user_id, session_id, request_header:<name>. Missing identifier behavior: assignment.fallback_key_behavior can be reject|random|anonymous_bucket. If reject, we return a 400 at config-creation time unless eligibility rules guarantee presence (not yet in scope), so in this PR reject is only allowed if allow_missing_identifiers=false is also set (to make intent explicit in Console). Basic cross-field constraints: hash_seed required if stickiness_key uses request_header; display_name length <= 80; description length <= 2k; owner_team required for enterprise auditability. Error messages Each validation error is returned with: a stable error code (EXPERIMENT_CONFIG_INVALID, EXP_ALLOCATION_SUM, EXP_VARIANT_DUP, EXP_STICKINESS_INVALID, etc.), a JSON pointer-style path (e.g. $.variants[1].allocation), and a concise message suitable for Console (no internal stack traces). Note: response shape is compatible with our existing Console “field error” rendering (same pattern as rollout policies). Storage & versioning Adds apiVersion v1alpha1 + schema version field to enable migrations. Persisted form includes schema_version=1 and a normalized config (sorted variants, normalized floats) to improve determinism across serialization boundaries. Tests Unit tests cover: allocation epsilon behavior, disabled variants, duplicate IDs, invalid stickiness key values, and max-variants guardrail. Golden tests for error messages to ensure Console copy stays stable across refactors. Non-goals / follow-ups Not included: policy engine evaluation (ENG-9022), config propagation/caching (ENG-9023), overlapping experiment validation (TBD; tracked in edge-cases doc), metrics attribution schema (ADR-048), and guardrail evaluation. Migration note This is additive and behind the route-experiments feature flag. No existing customer configs are affected. Release notes Internal only: Adds ExperimentConfig v1alpha1 schema and validation for route experiments; intended for beta enablement behind feature flag.
